title: Week 1: CodeIgniter Setup & Framework Intro
congratsText: Session 1 complete! You're ready for the lab.

sections.title: Session Objectives
sections.lesson:
Session Objectives
By the end of this session, you will be able to:
  • Explain what CodeIgniter is and why we use a framework over procedural PHP
  • Identify the key folders in the CodeIgniter project structure and their purpose
  • Describe what MVC means and how the three components connect
  • Trace a request from a browser URL all the way to HTML output
  • Configure config.php (base URL) and database.php for local development
  • Verify that CodeIgniter is installed and the welcome page loads correctly
What we are building toward: Over 16 weeks you will build a complete full-stack web application — backend with CodeIgniter (Weeks 1–8), then frontend with CSS, Bootstrap, and JavaScript (Weeks 9–16). This week lays the foundation.
Session structure: Work through all 10 sections in order. Section 9 is a recap. Take your time — each concept builds on the previous one.

sections.title: What is a PHP Framework?
sections.lesson:
What is a PHP Framework?
A PHP framework is a pre-built structure that gives your project a standard way to organize code, handle requests, and interact with the database.
Without a framework (procedural PHP):
  • You write everything from scratch — connection, queries, output all in one file
  • No enforced structure — files grow messy and hard to maintain quickly
  • Security and validation must be coded manually every single time
With a framework:
  • Structure is defined — you follow conventions the team already knows
  • Common tasks (DB connection, input handling, validation) are built-in
  • Security features like CSRF protection are included by default
Think of a framework as a building blueprint. Instead of designing the layout from scratch, you follow an established plan and focus on what makes your app unique.

quiz.question: What is the main advantage of using a PHP framework?
options: Frameworks make PHP apps run much faster
Frameworks provide structure and built-in tools
Frameworks eliminate writing any SQL at all here
Frameworks auto-deploy your project to production

sections.title: Why CodeIgniter?
sections.lesson:
Why CodeIgniter?
CodeIgniter 3 is a lightweight PHP framework known for its simplicity and gentle learning curve — an excellent first framework for developers who already know basic PHP.
Key reasons we use CodeIgniter in this course:
  • Simple to learn — minimal configuration to get started
  • Clear MVC structure — separates database, logic, and display into folders
  • Built-in security — CSRF protection, input filtering, SQL injection prevention
  • Configure once — set database credentials one time and use them in all models
  • Transferable knowledge — patterns here apply directly to Laravel, Symfony, and other frameworks
Once you understand CodeIgniter, learning any other framework becomes much easier — the core MVC concepts are the same everywhere.

quiz.question: Which CodeIgniter feature replaces mysqli_connect() on every page?
options: The built-in session management library handles it
The URL routing config file manages all connections
Configure the database once; all models share it
The automatic query builder removes the connection

sections.title: CodeIgniter Folder Structure
sections.lesson:
CodeIgniter Folder Structure
When you download CodeIgniter, you get a set of folders with specific purposes. Knowing where things go is the first step to working with the framework.
your_project/ ├── application/ ← YOUR code lives here │ ├── controllers/ ← Handle requests, call models │ ├── models/ ← All database code goes here │ ├── views/ ← HTML templates for output │ └── config/ ← config.php, database.php, etc. ├── system/ ← CodeIgniter core (don't touch!) ├── assets/ ← CSS, JS, images (you create this) └── index.php ← Entry point for all requests
The golden rule: You work almost entirely inside application/. The system/ folder is CodeIgniter's engine — never modify it.
  • controllers/ — one file per feature area (e.g., Students.php)
  • models/ — one file per database table (e.g., Student_model.php)
  • views/ — HTML files with embedded PHP for display only

quiz.question: Where do your own controllers, models, and views go?
options: Inside the system/ folder with the CI core files
Inside application/ in the matching subfolders
In the project root folder next to index.php here
Inside a new src/ folder you create from scratch

sections.title: MVC Architecture: The Big Picture
sections.lesson:
MVC Architecture: The Big Picture
MVC (Model-View-Controller) is the architectural pattern at the heart of CodeIgniter. It separates an application into three distinct responsibilities.
Component
  • Model
  • View
  • Controller
Responsibility
  • All database interaction
  • Display only — HTML output
  • Logic that connects M and V
Analogy — a restaurant:
  • Model = the kitchen — prepares and handles the food (data)
  • View = the dining area — presents the meal to the customer (HTML)
  • Controller = the waiter — takes the order, talks to kitchen, brings food out
This separation means you can change how data is displayed without touching database code — the key benefit of MVC.

quiz.question: Which MVC component handles all database interaction?
options: The View, because it displays data to users
The Controller, because it handles every request
The Model, because it encapsulates database code
The Router, because it maps URLs to tables

sections.title: The MVC Request Flow
sections.lesson:
The MVC Request Flow
Every request in CodeIgniter follows the same path. Understanding this flow is fundamental to working with any MVC framework.
Browser visits: http://localhost/students/index 1. index.php ← All requests pass through here first 2. Router ← Maps URL segments to controller + method 3. Controller ← students.php → index() method is called 4. Model ← Controller calls model to fetch data 5. Database ← Model runs query, returns result array 6. Controller ← Receives data back from the model 7. View ← Controller loads view and passes data 8. Browser ← View renders HTML and sends to user
The URL maps directly to PHP code:
http://localhost/[controller]/[method]/[parameter] /students/index → Students.php → index() /students/view/5 → Students.php → view($id=5) /products/delete/9 → Products.php → delete($id=9)
Notice: no file extensions in the URL — CodeIgniter handles all routing internally via index.php.

quiz.question: What does the URL /students/view/5 tell CodeIgniter?
options: Open the file students_view_5.php in views/
Run Students controller's view() with argument 5
Display row 5 from the students table directly
Search models/ for a method called students_view

sections.title: Configuring config.php
sections.lesson:
Configuring config.php
The file application/config/config.php holds global settings. The most critical one for local development is the base URL.
// Found in: application/config/config.php // Set this to match your local project folder $config['base_url'] = 'http://localhost/your_project_name/'; // Example for a project named 'school_system': $config['base_url'] = 'http://localhost/school_system/';
Why base_url matters:
  • Used by CodeIgniter helpers to build correct links and form actions
  • Incorrectly set → CSS files, links, and forms break across the site
  • Must match the exact folder name inside your WAMP/XAMPP htdocs/
  • Always include the trailing slash at the end
Other settings you may need:
  • $config['encryption_key'] — required if you use sessions
  • $config['index_page'] — set to '' to remove index.php from URLs

quiz.question: What breaks if base_url is set incorrectly in config.php?
options: PHP throws a fatal error and the page goes blank
Links, form actions, and asset paths break sitewide
The database connection silently fails each request
CodeIgniter switches to procedural mode by itself

sections.title: Configuring database.php
sections.lesson:
Configuring database.php
The file application/config/database.php stores your MySQL credentials. Configure it once and CodeIgniter uses it in every model automatically.
// Found in: application/config/database.php $db['default'] = array( 'hostname' => 'localhost', 'username' => 'root', 'password' => '', // Empty for local WAMP/XAMPP 'database' => 'school_db', // ← Change this to your DB name 'dbdriver' => 'mysqli', 'db_debug' => TRUE, // Show errors during development 'char_set' => 'utf8', );
The four fields you actually change:
  • hostname → almost always 'localhost'
  • username → usually 'root' on WAMP/XAMPP
  • password → empty string '' for local development
  • database → the name of your MySQL database
Compare: procedural PHP needs mysqli_connect() on every page. CI reads database.php once and shares it with all models.

quiz.question: What is the key advantage of configuring database.php once?
options: MySQL queries run faster through the CI database
All models share credentials without repeating them
The database.php file encrypts your password safely
CodeIgniter auto-creates the database from this file

sections.title: Procedural PHP vs CodeIgniter
sections.lesson:
Procedural PHP vs CodeIgniter: A Direct Comparison
Let's compare both approaches handling the same task: fetch and display all students.
// === PROCEDURAL PHP (one file does everything) === $conn = mysqli_connect("localhost", "root", "", "school_db"); $result = mysqli_query($conn, "SELECT * FROM students"); while ($row = mysqli_fetch_assoc($result)) { echo $row["name"] . "<br>"; } mysqli_close($conn);
// === CODEIGNITER (split across MVC) === // Model → Student_model.php public function get_all() { return $this->db->get('students')->result_array(); } // Controller → Students.php public function index() { $data['students'] = $this->Student_model->get_all(); $this->load->view('students/list', $data); } // View → students/list.php foreach ($students as $row) { echo $row['name'] . '<br>'; }
CodeIgniter means more files for the same output — but as your project grows to dozens of features, the separation keeps everything clean, testable, and maintainable.

quiz.question: What is the trade-off of using CodeIgniter over procedural PHP?
options: CodeIgniter pages render more slowly overall
More files upfront, but the project scales cleanly
CodeIgniter fully removes the need to learn SQL at all
Procedural PHP supports more database types than CI

sections.title: Session Recap
sections.lesson:
Session 1 Recap
Here is everything covered in this session at a glance:
  • PHP Framework — a structure that organizes code and provides built-in tools for common tasks
  • CodeIgniter — lightweight, beginner-friendly PHP framework; configure credentials once, use everywhere
  • Folder structure — your code lives in application/; never touch system/
  • MVC — Model (database) · View (HTML output) · Controller (logic that connects them)
  • Request flow — URL → Router → Controller → Model → Database → Controller → View → Browser
  • URL routing — /controller/method/parameter maps directly to PHP class methods
  • config.php — set base_url to your local project folder path with a trailing slash
  • database.php — set hostname, username, password, and database name once for all models
  • Procedural vs CI — more files and structure upfront, but cleaner and safer as the project grows
Lab activity (Week 1): Download and install CodeIgniter 3, configure both config files, and verify the welcome page loads correctly. See you in the lab!

quiz.question: Which items are central to a new CodeIgniter project setup?
options: index.php, system/, and the assets/ folder are core
application/, config.php, and database.php are key
controllers/, mysqli_connect(), and views/ come first
database.php, .htaccess, and system/ are set up first
